summary.text:
PowerBatch — microtask-coalescing dispatcher.
Collects items added within the same microtask and dispatches them
to the provided

. Useful to batch synchronous
work (DB writes, network calls) with minimal latency.
Function called with an array of collected items.
Number of items currently queued (not yet flushed).

Add an item to the current batch. Returns a Promise that resolves
when the batch containing this item has been processed. For non-flushed
additions this will be resolved after the microtask run; if adding the
item hits
the returned promise resolves when the handler completes.
Clear queued items without invoking handler.

Force flush the current queue immediately and return a promise
that resolves or rejects with the handler outcome.

PowerMemoizer

A small memoization wrapper backed by

.
It memoizes synchronous values and Promise-returning functions.
Concurrent calls for the same arguments are deduplicated (single inflight Promise).
Rejected Promises are not cached.

Usage (constructor returns a callable memoized function when given
Function that maps the wrapped call args to a cache key. Defaults to

is convenient but can be expensive for large or deeply-nested
  arguments. If the wrapped function is on a hot path, provide a custom

that cheaply and deterministically maps arguments to keys (for example, join simple
  scalar args with a separator or use a fast hashing function).

A small, fast key resolver for common cases where arguments are simple scalars.
- Fast path for primitive scalar args (string, number, boolean, null, undefined).
- Joins scalar args with

and prefixes type codes to avoid collisions.
- Falls back to

when any arg is a non-scalar (object, function, symbol).

This is intended as a performant default for hot paths where most calls use
simple identifiers (ids, numbers, short strings). It is deterministic but
not suitable for canonicalizing complex objects — provide a custom

to create a
helper that heuristically chunks an iterable and runs

for every item
inside lightweight inline worker-like instances managed by a

instance so callers can
interact with it (listen

Notes:
- This helper creates lightweight inline worker-like instances that execute

on each chunk element asynchronously (via
  setTimeout) so tests and environments without real Worker support still work.
- For heavy CPU work prefer a real Worker source string and create your own
instead; this helper focuses on convenience and correctness.

Manager for a pool of web workers.
A Worker constructor/factory (callable) or a relative path string to pass to

size.text: Initial number of workers to create.

minSize.text: Minimum number of workers to keep alive.

maxSize.text: Maximum number of workers allowed in the pool.

workerOptions.text: Options forwarded to the Worker constructor when using a string path.

maxTasksPerWorker.text: Soft capacity per worker before considering it busy.

taskQueue.text: Whether to queue tasks when all workers are busy.

lazy.text: If true, defer creating workers up to

summary.text: number of currently active (dispatched) tasks across all workers

Autoscale tick: simple policy that grows/shrinks by one worker based on
pool-level EWMA latency and queue pressure. Runs only when

is configured on the pool.

- scale up: when EWMA > targetMs OR queue length exceeds worker count
- scale down: when EWMA < targetMs * 0.5 AND queue is empty
whether the pool is considered idle (no active tasks and empty queue)
onerror handler called when a worker emits an error.
onidle handler called when the pool becomes idle.
onmessage handler called when any worker posts a message.

onresize handler called when the pool is resized and workers are terminated/added.
Receives an event object:
`{ data: { type: 'pool:resize', terminated: Array<number>, added: number, minSize, maxSize } }`
Add an event listener for pool events. Supported types: 'message', 'error', 'messageerror', 'idle'.
Add one worker to the pool immediately.
Broadcasts a message to all workers in the pool.

Shutdown the pool: clear timers, reject pending responses, terminate workers,
and clear internal queues. This is a full stop that prevents background
timers from keeping the process alive.

Stop all pending queued tasks and immediately post a message to the pool.
This clears the internal task queue first (cancelling pending tasks),
updates the pool idle state, then forwards the provided message using

so the message is dispatched to a live worker immediately
(or enqueued if no worker can accept it).
The message to post after clearing pending tasks.

and a plain object is supplied, the pool will attempt to encode the
object to a transferable

Stop the press and then post a batch of messages.

Clears the internal task queue and terminates inflight workers (optionally recreating them),
rejects pending response Promises, then forwards the provided batch to

.

This method mirrors the semantics of

for single messages but
operates on a batch. Use it when you need to atomically cancel pending work
and then seed the pool with a new set of tasks.

Lightweight Map-like store where each key has an optional TTL (milliseconds).
Entries expire lazily on access or iteration. Suitable when LRU/weighting
is unnecessary and a simple time-to-live map is desired.
